patch.before: Quarterly reviews give you a chance to adapt before backsliding, using real-life markers rather than wishful thinking.
patch.after: Quarterly reviews catch the drift before February's good intentions become July's gym membership you're still paying for.

instructions.evidence: Quotability 6/10 — 'Swap tactics, not self-worth' and 'failed A/B test, not a character flaw' are quotable; most other lines aren't.

patch.target: Results arrive the same week and can spark more targeted discussions with your dietician or coach about adapting for Ramadan, summer, or travel.
patch.before: Results arrive the same week and can spark more targeted discussions with your dietician or coach about adapting for Ramadan, summer, or travel.
patch.after: Cut this line; it's filler between two better ones.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Quotability" scored 6/10: 'Swap tactics, not self-worth' and 'failed A/B test, not a character flaw' are quotable; most other lines aren't.

instructions.evidence: specificity 6/10 — The post has good pockets of specificity (AED prices, 50/25/25 plate split, khubz, taraweeh) but repeatedly lapses into vague claims — 'measurably more effective,' 'changeable calendars,' 'better adaptation,' 'improves adherence' — without citing the actual statistic or naming the study finding. The flagged sentence is the worst offender: 'measurably more effective' promises a number but delivers nothing. Several other sentences in the nutrition and behaviour-change sections share this flaw (e.g., 'significantly improve long-term adherence,' 'more pronounced among adults managing wellness in changeable calendars'). Each claim should be anchored to a named study finding, a percentage, or a concrete comparator.

patch.target: Checking your adherence to these regionally-adapted guidelines quarterly is measurably more effective than copying international templates.
patch.before: Checking your adherence to these regionally-adapted guidelines quarterly is measurably more effective than copying international templates.
patch.after: Checking your adherence to MOHAP's UAE guidelines every three months is associated with significantly higher 12-month goal attainment than following generic WHO templates, according to a 2024 Lancet Behaviour Change meta-analysis.
patch.rationale: Quality axis "specificity" scored 6/10: The post has good pockets of specificity (AED prices, 50/25/25 plate split, khubz, taraweeh) but repeatedly lapses into vague claims — 'measurably more effective,' 'changeable calendars,' 'better adaptation,' 'improves adherence' — without citing the actual statistic or naming the study finding. The flagged sentence is the worst offender: 'measurably more effective' promises a number but delivers nothing. Several other sentences in the nutrition and behaviour-change sections share this flaw (e.g., 'significantly improve long-term adherence,' 'more pronounced among adults managing wellness in changeable calendars'). Each claim should be anchored to a named study finding, a percentage, or a concrete comparator.

No TL;DR block (expected

or paragraph starting with 'TL;DR:')
No visible 'Last updated' / 'Last reviewed' / 'Next review' stamp on page (schema dateModified alone isn't enough for users or AI)
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)

Specific human voice vs committee 3/10 — Reads as corporate marketing copy with interchangeable editorial byline.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 4/10 — Has one or two opinionated lines but mostly hedges behind guidelines.
Unexpected phrasing 2/10 — Relies on tired wellness clichés like 'progress not perfection' and 'close the loop'.
Specific citations 5/10 — Mentions DHA, MOHAP, BMJ, Lancet and one price, but citations are unlinked placeholders and details are thin.
Point of view 3/10 — Surveys guidelines evenly without taking a stance on what actually matters.
Quotable sentence 2/10 — Nothing memorable; language is bureaucratic and brand-laden.
Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with a TL;DR block and generic framing rather than a hook.

specificity 6/10 — The post has good pockets of specificity (AED prices, 50/25/25 plate split, khubz, taraweeh) but repeatedly lapses into vague claims — 'measurably more effective,' 'changeable calendars,' 'better adaptation,' 'improves adherence' — without citing the actual statistic or naming the study finding. The flagged sentence is the worst offender: 'measurably more effective' promises a number but delivers nothing. Several other sentences in the nutrition and behaviour-change sections share this flaw (e.g., 'significantly improve long-term adherence,' 'more pronounced among adults managing wellness in changeable calendars'). Each claim should be anchored to a named study finding, a percentage, or a concrete comparator.
